chunk_summary: Guidelines for inpatient disposition and discharge safety checks in India-focused rehabilitation settings, emphasizing structured monitoring, electrolyte stabilization, and nutrition planning for high-risk pediatric cases.
learning_objectives: Understand criteria for inpatient admission in pediatric nutrition rehabilitation, including severe dehydration, sepsis, and acute severe colitis.
Learn discharge safety protocols, including hydration stability, corrected electrolytes, and written nutrition prescriptions.
Recognize refeeding syndrome risks and implement monitored refeeding plans with staged calorie increases and lab monitoring.
key_facts: PUCAI scoring for ulcerative colitis: mild (10–34), moderate (35–64), severe (≥65).
Discharge requires stable hydration, corrected electrolytes, and a written nutrition plan.
Refeeding syndrome management includes thiamine prescription, staged-calorie plans, and lab monitoring within the first 72 hours.
Admission triggers include severe dehydration, shock, persistent vomiting, profuse diarrhea, and systemic toxicity.
Electrolyte abnormalities (low phosphate, potassium, magnesium) necessitate higher monitoring levels during refeeding.
reference_quote: For ulcerative colitis, pediatric guidance defines severity by PUCAI: mild 10–34, moderate 35–64, and severe ‡65; severe disease implies acute severe colitis requiring admission, structured monitoring, and time-based escalation rather than prolonged observation. (Source: ESPGHAN)
document_key_topics: Inpatient admission criteria for pediatric rehabilitation
Discharge safety protocols in nutrition rehabilitation
Management of refeeding syndrome in high-risk children
